text: The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position, approximately thirty degrees head down, with a bean bag placed underneath to prevent slipping during the robotic hysterectomy procedure.

The surgeon attaches the robotic arms to the surgical robot, ensuring precise positioning from the right side for the hysterectomy procedure. The arms are maneuvered into the optimal position before the procedure begins.

The video shows the uterus with visible fibroids. The surgeon has inserted a lateral right port and is preparing to introduce instruments. On the left side, the monopolar diathermy and Marylands forceps are visible.

The robotic arms maneuver into position, with monopolar scissors on the right side preparing to grasp the uterus. The surgeon uses the scissors to cut through the mesosalpines, removing the right fallopian tube while conserving the ovaries.

The surgeon uses scissors and monopolar diathermy to cut through the round ligament with minimal bleeding. The infundibular pelvic ligament is opened to expose the ovarian pedicles. The ovarian ligament is detached from the uterus, isolating the ovary, with the ovarian pedicle visible below.

The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy to coagulate the ovarian ligament, minimizing blood loss due to its vascular nature. Scissors are then used to cut through and open the ovarian pedicle, nearly separating the ovary. The ureters have been identified, and careful dissection continues to open the space and expose the uterine arteries on the right side. The round ligament on the uterine side is bleeding slightly, aiding visibility and reducing blood loss. The robotic system provides precise visualization of vessels, with the ureter visible far from the surgical site.

The surgeon uses a vaginal manipulator to facilitate the removal of the bladder peritoneum, pushing the bladder away to expose the uterine vessels. The scissors are positioned above the uterine vessel, preparing for diathermy application.

The surgeon uses Marylands to grasp the uterine artery on the right side, ensuring there is no bleeding. The procedure then shifts to the left side, where the surgeon navigates around the descending bowel and ovaries, which can be challenging due to their proximity to the ureters. An anterior wall fibroid obstructs the view of the bladder, complicating the visualization.

The surgeon separates the bladder peritoneum, carefully pushing the bladder away to expose the vagina below. The camera is maneuvered to provide a clear view of the anatomical space between the bladder, uterus, and vagina.

The robotic arms carefully push the bladder away, revealing the exposed vagina below. The surgeon uses scissors to cut into the vagina onto the Rumi II vaginal ring, while the bladder catheter is visible just above the scissors.

The surgeon opens the area around the uterine arteries, creating exposure before addressing the arteries themselves. Smoke is visible due to the use of monopolar cautery. The fibroid presents a challenge, with bowel visible on the right side, requiring careful avoidance of electrical conduction to prevent bowel injury. The green color of the vaginal manipulator is visible below, confirming correct positioning. As the view zooms in, the anterior space opens up clearly.

The surgeon uses the vaginal manipulator as a guide to perform the incision on the vaginal cuff, entering the pouch of Douglas. The uterus is anti-versed, and the manipulator is visible. The incision is made between the uterosacral ligaments, exposing the green Rumi II ring. The uterine arteries are visible to the left, and the surgeon carefully works across the vaginal cuff to expose and skeletalize the uterine vessels using a monopole and bipolar Marylands for diathermy, ensuring no ureters are in sight.

The surgeon exposes and skeletalizes the large uterine vein adjacent to the fibroid, preparing it for diathermy. The uterine vessels are visible and appear enlarged due to the presence of fibroids. The assistant uses suction to clear smoke, ensuring a clear view of the surgical field. The surgeon carefully works on the anterior vaginal wall, fully detaching the uterus from surrounding vessels and tissue, noting its anaemic appearance. The proximity of the bowel is visible, requiring caution with electrical energy.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to push and pull the uterus through the vaginal canal, utilizing the natural orifice for removal. The assistant aids in the process, ensuring the uterus, which may require debulking due to its size, is gradually extracted. The robotic system maintains intra-abdominal pressure effectively during the procedure.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to suture the vaginal vault after the uterus has been removed. The suturing involves taking the uterosacral ligament to provide support at the top of the vault. An OV lock suture is used, which is longer than usual, making the process more challenging. The robotic arms facilitate precise and efficient suturing within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon closes the vaginal vault by attaching the uterosacral ligaments to the vault, using a few needle throws in the relatively small space. Most bleeding has stopped, with remnants of blood visible from the procedure. Care is taken to avoid grabbing the bladder to prevent visco-vaginal fistulas.

A swab is placed below to minimize gas escape during suturing. The robotic arms are used to efficiently place sutures in the closed vaginal vault. The needle holder with a cutting device is passed through an eight-millimeter port, and the assistant retrieves it from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon expertly removes the needle through the port without bending it. Surgiflow is applied generously over the vaginal vault and peritoneal edges to reduce arterial bleeding and prevent vault hematoma, enhancing post-operative outcomes.
